theorem_title: Weak maximum principle via the negative part
theorem_statement:
Weak maximum principle via the negative part.

Let \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d\) be a bounded smooth domain, and let \(u\) be a sufficiently regular function such that
\[
-\Delta u \ge 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega,
\]
and
\[
u \ge 0 \quad \text{on } \partial \Omega.
\]
Then
\[
u \ge 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega.
\]

nodes.title: Weak maximum principle via the negative part
nodes.informal_statement: If \(-\Delta u \ge 0\) in \(\Omega\) and \(u \ge 0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), then \(u \ge 0\) in \(\Omega\).
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Test the inequality against the negative part \(u_-(x)=\max\{-u(x),0\}\). Since \(u_-\) vanishes on \(\partial\Omega\), integration by parts gives \(\int_\Omega (-\Delta u)u_-\,dx=\int_\Omega 
abla u\cdot
abla u_-\,dx\ge 0\). On \(\{u<0\}\), \(
abla u_-=-
abla u\), while on \(\{u\ge 0\}\), \(
abla u_-=0\), so \(\int_\Omega 
abla u\cdot
abla u_-\,dx=-\int_\Omega |
abla u_-|^2\,dx\). Hence \(\int_\Omega |
abla u_-|^2\,dx\le 0\), so \(u_-\) is constant; the boundary condition forces that constant to be zero. Therefore \(u_\equiv 0\), i.e. \(u\ge 0\) in \(\Omega\).

nodes.title: Testing with the negative part and integration by parts
nodes.informal_statement:
For \(u_-(x)=\max\{-u(x),0\}\), one has \(u_-\ge 0\) in \(\Omega\), \(u_-=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), and
\[
\int_\Omega (-\Delta u)\,u_-\,dx = \int_\Omega \nabla u\cdot\nabla u_-\,dx.
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text: Because \(u\ge 0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), the negative part \(u_-\) has zero boundary trace. Thus it is an admissible test function for the weak form of \(-\Delta u \ge 0\), and integration by parts removes the boundary term.
nodes.formalization_rationale: This packages the admissibility of \(u_-\) as a test function and the boundary-term cancellation, which is a standard but formally delicate step in weak maximum principle proofs.

nodes.title: Energy identity for the negative part
nodes.informal_statement:
The gradients satisfy
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u\cdot\nabla u_-\,dx = -\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx.
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text:
On \(\{u\ge 0\}\), one has \(u_-=0\) and hence \(
abla u_-=0\). On \(\{u<0\}\), \(u_-=-u\), so \(
abla u_-=-
abla u\). Therefore \(
abla u\cdot
abla u_-=-|
abla u_-|^2\) pointwise where \(u<0\), and the identity follows after integrating over \(\Omega\).
nodes.formalization_rationale:
This is the main local formal island: the pointwise case split on \(u\ge 0\) vs. \(u<0\) produces the decisive energy identity \(\int 
abla u\cdot
abla u_- = -\int |
abla u_-|^2\).

nodes.title: Vanishing of the negative part
nodes.informal_statement: If \(\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx\le 0\), then \(u_-=0\) in \(\Omega\), hence \(u\ge 0\) in \(\Omega\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: Since \(|\nabla u_-|^2\ge 0\), the integral inequality forces \(|\nabla u_-|^2=0\) almost everywhere. Thus \(u_-\) is constant on \(\Omega\); the boundary condition \(u_-=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\) forces that constant to be zero. Consequently \(u_-\equiv 0\), so \(u\) never takes negative values.
nodes.formalization_rationale: This is the final deduction from the nonpositive energy integral to \(u_-\equiv 0\), combining nonnegativity, constancy, and the boundary trace condition.

edges.label: compute
edges.explanation: The integration-by-parts identity is sharpened by identifying the integrand in terms of \(u_-\).

edges.label: sign argument
edges.explanation: The identity yields a nonpositive integral of a nonnegative quantity, forcing \(\nabla u_- = 0\) a.e.
